old_claim.note: Mechanical Clocks provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Clockwork-style spring, sear, tumbler, cock, and frizzen lockwork supplies the mechanical subsystem that distinguishes true flintlock firearms from earlier matchlock or hand-cannon lineages.

source_shape.source_locator: The Flintlock Mechanism section identifies the hammer/cock, mainspring, frizzen, pan, tumbler, sear, sear spring, and frizzen spring, then explains how trigger release drives the hammer and spark sequence.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes the flintlock as a spring-driven lock mechanism with sear/tumbler release and coordinated cock, frizzen, pan, and spring parts.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This favors the clockwork_mechanisms prerequisite because that node covers precision spring-and-lock mechanical craft, while mechanical_clocks would imply timekeeping devices rather than the lock subsystem.

ontology_before: The node pointed at Mechanical Clocks, implying that timekeeping clocks directly enabled flintlock firearms.
ontology_after: The node points at Clockwork Mechanisms, preserving the mechanical craft dependency while removing the overly broad timekeeping-clock claim.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: muskets_flintlock now depends on clockwork_mechanisms and no longer directly depends on mechanical_clocks.
would_reject_if: A stronger source showed the flintlock mechanism did not use spring-and-sear lockwork.
The clockwork_mechanisms node were narrowed only to clocks and excluded non-clock spring mechanisms.
A source showed mechanical clockmaking as a named direct prerequisite rather than a broader mechanical-craft analogy.
short_reason: Flintlocks need lockwork mechanics, not clocks as timekeeping devices.
